title: The Two-Stage Tradeoff
concept: Using fast bi-encoders to narrow millions of documents to dozens, then slow cross-encoders to rerank those dozens precisely.

exercises.prompt: Why can't we index and search millions of documents ahead of time using a Cross-encoder?

options.text: They are too large to fit in modern server memory.
They need the user's query at inference time to compute the joint attention score.
They only work with image data, not text documents.

exercises.explain: Cross-encoders cannot pre-calculate document embeddings because they require both the user's query and the document to be fed into the network together at the moment of searching.

cards.title: The Best of Both Worlds
cards.body: If you want the absolute highest quality search, you should use a Cross-encoder. But if you have millions of documents, doing this directly is way too slow. What if we could combine the speed of Bi-encoders with the intelligence of Cross-encoders?

cards.title: Two-Stage Retrieval
cards.body: A two-stage retrieval pipeline uses a fast Bi-encoder to retrieve a small candidate set (e.g., 50 documents) from a massive dataset, and then uses a precise Cross-encoder to rerank only those 50 candidates. This gives you the speed of vector search combined with the deep understanding of cross-attention.

cards.title: The Hiring Recruiter Analogy
cards.body: Think of it like hiring a new team member. The Bi-Encoder is the HR recruiter who quickly filters 10,000 resumes down to 10 candidates based on keywords. The Cross-Encoder is the hiring manager who conducts deep, 1-hour interviews with just those 10 candidates to pick the absolute best one.

cards.title: Search in Action
cards.body: When you search for 'how to fix a leaky faucet' on a modern search engine, a Bi-encoder quickly grabs the top 100 plumbing articles using vector distance. Then, a Cross-encoder analyzes the exact phrasing of your query alongside those 100 articles, bubble-sorting the absolute best step-by-step guide to the #1 spot.

cards.title: The False Dichotomy
cards.body: Many developers think they have to choose between a fast search system and an accurate one. In reality, modern search systems use both in sequence, proving you don't have to sacrifice quality for speed.

cards.title: The Pipeline Flow
cards.body: Visualizing the pipeline workflow from a massive dataset to the final top results.

left.text: Searches 1,000,000 documents instantly using vector similarity. Outputs top 100.

right.text: Reranks the 100 documents using deep attention. Outputs top 10 highly accurate results.

exercises.prompt: Which model should you use in the first stage to narrow down 1,000,000 documents to 100?

options.text: A Bi-encoder, because it can query pre-computed vector indexes in milliseconds.
A Cross-encoder, because it compares the query to every document in parallel.
A manual database scan, because it doesn't require ML models.
A Cross-encoder, because it is more accurate for large datasets.

exercises.explain: Bi-encoders are fast enough to search millions of documents in milliseconds using vector indexing, making them the perfect 'first stage' filter.

exercises.prompt: True or False: We should use a Cross-encoder to score all 5 million documents in our database for every user query.
exercises.explain: Cross-encoders must process the query and document together at the same time, making them too slow to run on millions of documents for every search query.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the two-stage retrieval process:
exercises.sentence: We first retrieve candidate documents, and then use a Cross-encoder to rerank them.
exercises.explain: In a two-stage system, we first retrieve a small pool of candidate documents, and then use the Cross-encoder to rerank them for the final output.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following search results highlights the deep query-document analysis typical of an AI-powered Cross-encoder reranker?

options.text: A basic keyword match that returns documents containing the word 'java' sorted by date.
A simple vector similarity search that returns the closest 10 articles based on title embeddings.
A reranked list of documents where the system analyzed the query 'java basics' against paragraph contents to prioritize introductory programming concepts over coffee brewing guides.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an AI generated search result ranking because it shows a deep, non-linear comparison of semantic intent (matching 'java' programming concepts to 'object-oriented language' without needing direct keyword matches).

exercises.prompt: You are building a search engine for 10 million user posts. Users expect search results in under 200 milliseconds, but you also need high-quality relevance. How should you design the pipeline?

options.text: Retrieve the top 100 documents using a Bi-encoder, then rerank them using a Cross-encoder.
Compare all 1 million documents directly using a Cross-encoder to ensure absolute accuracy.
Run a Bi-encoder on the entire database twice to double the retrieval quality without using Cross-encoders.
Use a Cross-encoder to index the database overnight and a Bi-encoder to search it at runtime.

exercises.explain: By combining the speed of Bi-encoders with the accuracy of Cross-encoders in a two-stage pipeline, you get the best of both worlds: lightning-fast search over millions of documents and highly accurate relevance ranking for the top results.
